full_response:
Let the parameter λ vary in the open interval 0<λ<1 and define the Cartesian components of the curve v(λ)⊂ℝ³ by  

x(λ)=sin(λ²), y(λ)=ln(λ+1), z(λ)=2λ²−1.  (1)

To express the same curve in the usual spherical coordinates (r,θ,φ) chosen so that  

x=r sinθ cosφ, y=r sinθ sinφ, z=r cosθ,  (2)

with 0≤θ≤π and −π<φ≤π, we compute r, θ, and φ as explicit functions of λ.

1. Radial coordinate r(λ)  
   r(λ)=√[x²+y²+z²]  
        =√[sin²(λ²)+ln²(λ+1)+(2λ²−1)²].  (3)

2. Polar angle θ(λ)  
   cosθ = z/r ⇒ θ(λ)=arccos[(2λ²−1)/r(λ)].  (4)

3. Azimuthal angle φ(λ)  
   tanφ = y/x (because x>0, y>0 for 0<λ<1, the curve always lies in the first quadrant of the xy-plane, so φ∈(0,π/2) and arctan is single-valued)  

   φ(λ)=arctan[ ln(λ+1)/sin(λ²) ].  (5)

Collecting (3)–(5):

v(λ) in spherical form (0<λ<1) is  

r(λ)=√[sin²(λ²)+ln²(λ+1)+(2λ²−1)²],  
θ(λ)=arccos\!\left[\dfrac{2λ²-1}{\sqrt{\,\sin²(λ²)+\ln²(λ+1)+(2λ²-1)²}}\right],  
φ(λ)=\arctan\!\left[\dfrac{\ln(λ+1)}{\sin(λ²)}\right],  0<λ<1.  

\boxed{\;v(λ)=\bigl(r(λ),\,θ(λ),\,φ(λ)\bigr)\;\text{with}\;r,θ,φ\;\text{given above}\;} 

Confidence: 86%
